name: Fernley Hills
short_description: BLM public rockhounding site in Lyon County featuring agate and chalcedony specimens. Easy access with gentle slopes and excellent collecting potential for beginners and experienced rockhounds.
description:
Fernley Hills is a scenic BLM rockhounding area in Lyon County, Nevada, offering excellent opportunities to collect agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. The site sits at 5167 feet elevation with gentle rolling terrain that makes it accessible to rockhounds of varying skill levels. The combination of open desert landscape and exposed geological formations creates ideal conditions for surface collecting.

This area lies within Nevada's Basin and Range province, where Miocene-era volcanic activity and subsequent hydrothermal alteration created silica-rich deposits that formed the agate nodules and chalcedony specimens found throughout the Fernley Hills today. The volcanic rhyolite and basalt parent rocks weathered and fractured over millions of years, exposing the gem-quality material at the surface.

Collectors can expect to find colorful agate pieces with banding patterns, translucent chalcedony specimens, and jasper fragments scattered across hillsides and wash areas. Many specimens are small to medium-sized, making them ideal for cabochon cutting, jewelry making, or display collections. The gentle slope terrain means you can cover significant ground without extreme physical exertion.

As BLM public land, Fernley Hills welcomes recreational rockhounders under Nevada's personal use guidelines. The open access and lack of permit requirements make this an excellent choice for weekend collecting trips. Best results come from systematic searching along exposed ridges and in dry washes where water erosion has concentrated lighter specimens.
history: The Fernley Hills area sits within Nevada's highly mineralized Basin and Range geologic province, where Miocene volcanic eruptions (approximately 20-10 million years ago) deposited extensive silica-rich fluids through fractures in the underlying bedrock. These hydrothermal systems created the agate nodules and chalcedony veins that rockhounders pursue today, with secondary weathering and erosion bringing specimens to the surface over the past several million years.
tips: Search along the gentle hillside ridges and in dry wash beds where wind and occasional runoff have concentrated lighter-colored specimens and cleared overlying soil. Bring a small hand lens to identify chalcedony's waxy luster and agate's fine banding patterns in the field, which helps distinguish quality specimens from common quartz. Focus on areas with exposed rhyolite outcrops showing reddish or purple tones, as these host the best agate and chalcedony deposits. Early morning or late afternoon light makes it easier to spot the translucent edges of chalcedony pieces lying on dark volcanic soil.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 5167 feet with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Open desert terrain with sparse sagebrush and desert vegetation. Rocky volcanic soil with scattered dark basalt and rhyolite fragments. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas typically ranges from 50 feet to one-half mile. Footing is generally stable but rocky, requiring sturdy boots. The gentle slope minimizes elevation gain while walking.
best_season: October - April. Nevada's high desert location means summer temperatures regularly exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit, making midday collecting uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. Fall through spring offers cooler temperatures (50-70 degrees), lower UV exposure, and occasional moisture that helps reveal specimen colors. Winter months are mild at this 5167-foot elevation, though early morning temperatures may drop below freezing.
written_directions: From Fernley (approximately 15 miles northeast of Fernley Hills site), take Nevada State Route 95A south toward the Fernley Hills area. Continue for approximately 8-10 miles until you reach access points along BLM land marked by open desert terrain and exposed rocky ridges. Look for obvious pullouts on either side of the highway where vehicle tracks lead into the hillsides. GPS coordinates 39.53364, -119.156639 mark the primary collecting zone. A standard passenger vehicle can access the main parking areas, though high-clearance vehicles are recommended for exploring deeper into the site.
road_conditions: Paved highway access with graded dirt pullouts and two-track roads leading into collecting areas. Some rougher sections may have washboard texture but remain passable to passenger vehicles. High-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicles better suited for exploring remote sections.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along the hillsides with capacity for 3-5 vehicles per location. No developed parking lot facilities. Pullouts range from small turnouts to slightly larger flat areas adjacent to productive collecting zones.
collection_rules: Personal use only under BLM regulations. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection or sale of specimens. No motorized excavation or use of power tools. Hand collecting and light rock breaking with hammer and chisel permitted. Removal of specimens from the ground surface only, no digging required.
rules:
Personal use only
25 pound daily limit per person
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
Hand tools and small hammers permitted
Surface collecting only - no excavation
Pack out all trash
Leave gates as you found them
nearest_services: Fernley, Nevada (approximately 15 miles northwest) offers gas stations, restaurants, and grocery stores. Fallon, Nevada (approximately 35 miles south) provides additional services and medical facilities.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved dirt pullouts and two-track roads to parking areas. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp volcanic rock and loose gravel. No wheelchair accessibility on collecting trails. Most productive areas within short walking distance of parking (under one-half mile).

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Fernley Hills near Fernley, Nevada?
faq.answer: Fernley Hills is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. The colorful agate pieces often feature banding patterns ideal for cutting cabochons, while translucent chalcedony specimens are prized for jewelry making. Most specimens are small to medium-sized and found on the surface.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Fernley Hills BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Fernley Hills. As BLM public land in Nevada, the site welcomes personal use collecting under FLPMA guidelines. Simply follow the 25-pound daily limit and hand-collection rules to stay within regulations.

faq.question: How to find agate at Fernley Hills rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Search along exposed hillside ridges and in dry wash beds where water erosion concentrates specimens. Look for areas with reddish or purple rhyolite outcrops, as these host the best agate deposits. Use a hand lens to identify the fine banding patterns that distinguish quality agate from common quartz.

faq.question: Is Fernley Hills rockhounding site beginner friendly and family-suitable?
faq.answer: Yes, Fernley Hills is excellent for beginners and families. The gentle slopes require minimal physical exertion, specimens are visible on the surface without excavation needed, and the easy access from paved highways makes it a perfect weekend collecting trip for all skill levels.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding near Fernley, Nevada?
faq.answer: October through April offers ideal collecting conditions at Fernley Hills. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees and pose heat danger, while fall through spring provides comfortable 50-70 degree temperatures. Winter months are mild at the 5167-foot elevation, though mornings may drop below freezing.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at Fernley Hills Lyon County?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand lens for identifying chalcedony's waxy luster and agate banding in the field. Pack a rock hammer and chisel for light breaking of nodules, sturdy boots for rocky terrain, sun protection including hat and sunscreen, plenty of water (minimum 2-3 liters), and a collection bag. A four-wheel-drive vehicle is optional but helpful for exploring remote areas.

meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony, and jasper at Fernley Hills, an easy-access BLM rockhounding site near Fernley, Nevada. No permit required. Collect up to 25 lbs daily.
